candidate_rule.rule: When a lesion displays a central area of mixed brown-pink-violaceous hues with a 'moth-eaten' or finely granular, loosely arranged texture (resembling milia-like cysts or comedo-like openings dispersed within a structureless zone), surrounded by a peripheral halo of faint brownish pigment network fading into normal skin — without arborizing telangiectasias, leaf-like areas, or spoke-wheel structures — classify as Benign Keratosis.
preconditions: Central zone shows mixed brown, pink, and violaceous coloration without discrete nodular or pearly quality
Texture within the central zone is finely granular, loosely structured, or moth-eaten in appearance — consistent with milia-like cysts or comedone-like openings characteristic of seborrheic keratosis
Peripheral rim shows diffuse, faint brownish pigmentation that gradually fades into the surrounding skin (no sharp, well-demarcated border typical of BCC)
Absence of arborizing (branching) telangiectasias — the hallmark vascular feature of Basal Cell Carcinoma
Absence of leaf-like areas, spoke-wheel structures, or blue-gray ovoid nests characteristic of BCC
Overall lesion architecture is flat-to-slightly-raised without a rolled or pearlescent border
candidate_rule.rationale: Benign keratosis (seborrheic keratosis or lichenoid keratosis) frequently presents with mixed brown-violaceous-pink hues and a granular or moth-eaten internal texture due to milia-like cysts and comedo openings. The peripheral pigment fades gradually rather than forming the sharp, translucent rolled border of BCC. The critical distinguishing feature is the absence of arborizing telangiectasias and blue-gray ovoid nests that define BCC. When the model encounters this mixed-pigment granular pattern without BCC-specific vascular or structural features, it should favor Benign Keratosis over BCC.

candidate_rule.rule: When a small, ill-defined pigmented lesion on hairy skin shows a moth-eaten or milia-like central aggregation of brown-to-dark granules arranged in a loosely clustered, non-structured pattern WITHOUT arborizing vessels, spoke-wheel structures, or leaf-like areas, and the surrounding skin displays prominent terminal hairs and diffuse pink-red vascular blush consistent with normal skin, classify as Benign Keratosis.
preconditions: Small pigmented lesion set within hairy skin background with multiple terminal hairs visible across the field
Central zone composed of loosely aggregated brown and dark-brown granules or clods with no sharp geometric organization
Absence of arborizing (tree-like) telangiectatic vessels — the hallmark of BCC
Absence of spoke-wheel areas, leaf-like structures, or blue-gray ovoid nests characteristic of BCC
Diffuse pink-to-light-red background blush consistent with normal vascularized skin rather than BCC's pearly or translucent quality
No ulceration, blue-white veil, or shiny white structures (chrysalis) visible
Lesion borders are ill-defined and fading rather than raised or pearly-edged
candidate_rule.rationale: Basal Cell Carcinoma requires at least one of its specific dermoscopic criteria (arborizing vessels, blue-gray ovoid nests, spoke-wheel structures, leaf-like areas, ulceration, or shiny white strands) to be diagnosed. This lesion on hairy skin shows only loosely aggregated brown granules — a pattern consistent with a flat seborrheic keratosis or lentigo-type benign keratosis — without any of the obligate BCC features. The hairy-skin context and the granular, non-structured pigment pattern strongly favor a benign keratosis. Models may hesitate because the dark central clod mimics a BCC nest, but the complete absence of BCC-specific vascular and structural patterns should tip classification toward Benign Keratosis.
